it's less about convincing the AEUG he isn't Char Aznable than it is convincing himself that he doesn't have to meet the expectations of being Char Aznable. Zeta begins Char desiring to abandon all expectations, and focusing solely on fighting for a cause he believes in

I certainly think Char spends a huge amount of time lying in both Zeta and CCA. In Zeta, mostly to himself, however. Even the Quattro Bajeena persona is aimed far more at himself than the rest of the AEUG;

It ends with him realizing both that the cause is doomed, and that he has to take on those expectations. Throughout Zeta, he's continuously pushed to assume more and more responsibility, to be Char Aznable. As far as he's concerned, he's finally being true to himself.

He has to be Char Aznable, he has to be Casval Rem Deikun. But neither of these things are true; he's only ever playing a role, even if he hardly knows it himself. The "real" Char, the Char he would be if not saddled with the expectations of his bloodline, isn't a leader. https://t.co/twitter_link

That he can act freely. In the last moments of CCA, Char is going to die. He knows this. He knows the person he is talking to is going to die. He can finally speak freely, without needing to adhere to expectation; and he doesn't. Not at first, anyways. He keeps up the bluster,

CCA Char, certainly, is an extremely fake persona. He has become what the people around him expect him to be, only ever acting in ways that meet their expactations. He allows himself minor moments of vulnerability in private with Nanai, but it isn't until the end of the movie

until the topic of Quess comes up, and how Char manipulated her by using Quess's belief that Char could be a father figure to her. This triggers Char's last words; not a defence of himself or his actions, not a realization that what he's done was wrong, or a display of contrition

Char talks about himself, completely unrelated to the matter at hand. He has zero reason to lie here. For the first time this movie, he is being real. And what does he say? "Lalah was a woman who could've been like a mother to me".
Cover art reveal today or monday?

I think the idea that Char is lying there is kinda silly. I also find the idea that Char is a "psychopath" to be similarly silly; he lies, but not out of any pathological need to. He is entirely a man of circumstances, molded from the day he was born.

Lalah was the one person to view Char without expectation. He was not the Red Comet, the ace pilot, or Casval Rem Deikun, the saviour of the spacenoids. He was someone she loved, unconditionally. That's all Char wanted; unconditional love, the reassurance of a mother figure.
He genuinely follows a path he mostly believes in, but he allows it to be molded by those around him; even as a leader, he doesn't lead. He conforms to fit the expectations of those around him.
This is all kinda rambling but idk. Many thoughts on Char Aznable.
